questions.question: Explain the difference between the capacity and size of a dynamic array. When is resizing triggered?
keyPoints: Size is number of actual elements stored; capacity is total allocated slots
Resizing triggered when load factor exceeds threshold (typically 0.75)
May shrink when load factor drops below threshold (typically 0.25)

questions.question: What is amortized analysis? Why is insertion at the end of a dynamic array called O(1) amortized?
keyPoints: Amortized analysis averages worst-case costs over sequence of operations
Occasional O(n) resize is rare compared to many O(1) insertions
Total cost of n insertions is O(n), giving O(1) amortized per insertion

questions.question: Compare dynamic arrays and linked lists for the following operations: random access, insertion at beginning, insertion at end.
keyPoints: Random access: Dynamic array O(1), Linked list O(n)
Insertion at beginning: Dynamic array O(n), Linked list O(1)
Insertion at end: Both O(1) amortized for dynamic array, O(1) for linked list with tail pointer

questions.question: What is the load factor of a dynamic array? Explain the role of load factor in triggering resize operations.
keyPoints: Load factor = number of elements / capacity
Grow threshold typically 0.75: resize up when exceeded
Shrink threshold typically 0.25: resize down when below
Purpose: balance between memory usage and resizing frequency

questions.question: Explain why doubling the capacity (growth factor of 2) provides amortized O(1) insertion time.
keyPoints: Total cost of all resize operations = n + n/2 + n/4 + ... < 2n = O(n)
Cost of n insertions including resizing = O(n)
Average (amortized) cost per insertion = O(n)/n = O(1)

questions.question: What is shrink-to-fit in dynamic arrays? When is it typically performed and why is it important?
keyPoints: Shrink-to-fit reduces capacity when load factor drops below threshold (e.g., 0.25)
Important to free unused memory
Prevents memory waste when many deletions occur
May cause iterator invalidation

questions.question: List three advantages and three disadvantages of dynamic arrays.
keyPoints: Advantages: O(1) random access, cache-friendly contiguous memory, efficient for iteration
Disadvantages: O(n) insertion/deletion in middle, costly resize operations, may waste memory

questions.question: Describe the process of inserting an element at the end of a dynamic array. Include steps for both when space is available and when resizing is required. Also explain the time complexity of this operation.
keyPoints: Check if size equals capacity (array full)
If not full: add element at index size, increment size, O(1)
If full: allocate new array (typically 2x capacity), copy all elements to new array, deallocate old array, add element, update capacity
Amortized O(1) time complexity explanation using aggregate analysis
Resize cost distributed over all insertions

questions.question: Implement a dynamic array (vector) with the following operations: insert at end, delete from end, resize, and get current size and capacity. Explain each operation with pseudocode and analyze their time complexities.
keyPoints: Insert at end: Check capacity, resize if needed, add element at size++, O(1) amortized
Delete from end: Decrement size, may shrink if load factor < threshold, O(1)
Resize operation: Allocate new array of given size, copy elements, update capacity
Get size and capacity: Return current size and capacity values, O(1)
Space complexity discussion and trade-offs

questions.question: Explain the concept of iterator invalidation in dynamic arrays. Under what conditions do iterators become invalid? Why is this important for programmers?
keyPoints: Iterator points to memory location of element
When array resizes, elements move to new memory location
All iterators invalidated on any resize operation
After insertion/deletion at position before iterator, iterator becomes invalid
Important because using invalidated iterators causes undefined behavior
Programmers must be careful when storing and using iterators across modifications

questions.question: Compare dynamic arrays with linked lists. When would you choose one over the other? Provide specific use cases for each.
keyPoints: Dynamic arrays: O(1) access, cache-friendly, good for iteration and random access
Linked lists: O(1) insertion/deletion at any position, no wasted memory
Choose dynamic array for: random access patterns, iteration, sorted data with binary search
Choose linked list for: frequent insertions/deletions at arbitrary positions, unknown size
Use cases: Array for database records (random access), List for undo operations (varying positions)
